nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; tends to hover above coral heads, inside caves, and in shaded reef areas at depths of roughly 3–46 m.

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on small zooplankton, copepods, brine shrimp, frozen mysis shrimp, and other small meaty preparations. Generally advised to offer small amounts several times daily; frozen vitamin-enriched foods are commonly recommended.

imageCandidates.notes: Robin White; 1670x1252; two individuals in-situ over reef rubble, sharp, clearly shows orange facial mask, dark eye, translucent body and silver lateral dashes — group shot suits this shoaling species.
Dan Schofield; 2048x1535; striking tack-sharp lone specimen with clean bokeh, vivid orange stripe and translucent body against a red reef backdrop.
Pauline Walsh Jacobson; 960x720; vibrant classic side profile, translucent orange body with silver dashes and dark masked eye; small corner watermark.

summary: A tiny, translucent Caribbean reef goby with orange facial markings. Generally considered reef-safe and peaceful; tends to hover in open water above coral heads and caves. Captive-bred specimens are sometimes available.
careNotes: The Masked Goby is generally considered one of the easier nano reef fish to keep. In the wild it tends to occur in schools near coral columns and caves throughout the tropical western Atlantic, ranging from Florida and Bermuda through the Bahamas and into northern South America. In an aquarium, providing plenty of live rock with caves and overhangs is generally advised, as individuals naturally gravitate toward shaded areas and hover above the bottom rather than resting on substrate. While it can be kept singly, groups of three or more tend to show more natural schooling behavior; a tank of at least 75 L is recommended to comfortably house a small group. This species is a protogynous hermaphrodite (meaning it begins life as female and the dominant individual in a group eventually transitions to male). Captive breeding has been reported, with spawning observed as early as 100 days post-hatching under aquaculture conditions, though breeding in a home aquarium is generally considered moderately challenging. A tightly-fitted lid is advisable given the small size of this fish and the tendency of gobies as a group to jump.
breedingNotes: The Masked Goby is a protogynous hermaphrodite — it begins life as female, and the dominant individual in a group will eventually transition to male. Spawning has been reported in captivity, including under aquaculture conditions where it has been observed as early as around 100 days post-hatching. Captive-bred specimens are commercially available through aquaculture producers. Breeding in a home aquarium is possible but has been described as somewhat more challenging than other small reef fish.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source for tank size (10 gal), temperature (72–78°F), salinity (1.020–1.025), pH (8.1–8.4), dKH (8–12), care level (Easy), and diet notes.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (4.0 cm TL), temperature range (24.9–28°C wild habitat), taxonomy (Gobiidae / Gobiiformes), and native range (Western Central Atlantic). Temperature min is higher than LiveAquaria's listing; final range averaged across both sources.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (4 cm), minimum tank volume (50 L), salinity (~1.026 SG), reef-safe status, and schooling/grouping behavior. Minimum tank size averaged between LiveAquaria (38 L) and ReefApp (50 L) then biased upward to 75 L to accommodate schooling groups.

additional.name: ORA (Oceans, Reefs & Aquariums)
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (~1.75 in / ~4.45 cm), protogynous hermaphroditism, schooling behavior, Caribbean origin, and captive breeding viability.

fish.breedingNotes: Protogynous hermaphrodites; captive spawning has been reported in aquaculture settings and advanced home reef setups. Captive-bred specimens are commercially available. Spawning has been observed around 100 days post-hatching.
